categoryTagline: The small things people notice before they notice the clothes.

picks.name: Slim Sleeve Wallet
picks.bio: The wallet that fixes the thing you didn't know was aging you — the fat, overstuffed brick in your back pocket. Slim leather, holds the cards and cash you actually use, and breaks in beautifully. The single easiest upgrade to how you carry yourself, and nobody will ever know it cost under a hundred bucks.
picks.flashyCheck: None. A slim, no-logo wallet is the quiet sign of a guy who's edited his life down.

picks.name: Aluminum Card Wallet
picks.bio: If you want bulletproof over leather, the Ridge is the one — machined metal, RFID-blocking, fans your cards out with a satisfying click. The honest caveat: it carries fewer cards than it looks like it should, and the cash strap is fiddly. But for a minimalist who wants the wallet to outlive the decade, it delivers.
picks.flashyCheck: Mild. It reads as deliberate and modern — just don't expect it to vanish in a pocket like leather.

picks.name: Wayfarer Classic
picks.bio: Still the answer after seventy years. The Wayfarer suits nearly every face, goes with everything, and reads as effortless precisely because it's never trying. If you own one pair of sunglasses, own these in black. The shape is so right that the entire sunglasses industry is just variations on it.
picks.flashyCheck: None — it's the opposite of flashy. A classic shape signals taste, not money.

picks.name: 714 Folding Sunglasses
picks.bio: Steve McQueen wore them for a reason — Italian-made, folding frames, and a quiet kind of cool that the cheaper shades can't fake. Expensive, yes, but they fold into a jacket pocket and last for years, and the people who recognize them are exactly the people whose opinion you'd care about. The grown-up's sunglasses.
picks.flashyCheck: Tasteful. A connoisseur detail — recognized by the few, never loud to the many.

picks.name: Sunglasses (Prescription-Ready)
picks.bio: The value play, especially if you need prescription lenses — sharp classic shapes, polarized options, and a price that doesn't hurt when you inevitably sit on a pair. Try-on-at-home makes getting the right fit easy. Not heritage, but genuinely good-looking and hard to fault for the money.
picks.flashyCheck: None. Clean and unbranded — it just looks like you chose well.

picks.name: Big-Logo Designer Shades — Skip Them
picks.bio: The honest read: big-logo designer sunglasses cost double a Ray-Ban to advertise the brand on the side of your head, and that's the whole product. The lenses and frames are often made in the same factories. If you've got $350 burning a hole, a pair of Wayfarers and a Persol 714 covers two looks that signal taste instead of spend. Logos on temples are a tell.
picks.flashyCheck: Maximum flash, minimum taste. The knowing move is the unbranded classic.

picks.name: Full-Grain Leather Dress Belt
picks.bio: One good leather belt beats five cheap ones, and this is the one — full-grain leather, a clean unbranded buckle, made to match real dress shoes. The rule nobody taught you: match the belt to the shoes, brown with brown, black with black. Buy this in both and you're covered for a decade.
picks.flashyCheck: None. A no-logo leather belt is pure quiet competence — you want the belt, not the billboard.
picks.productSearch: Allen Edmonds wide basic dress belt

picks.name: Small Rugged Twill Duffel
picks.bio: The weekend bag you hand down. Filson's tin-cloth and bridle leather are built to look better beaten up, and the small duffel is the right size for two or three nights without checking a bag. It's a real investment, but you'll be carrying this same bag in fifteen years while everyone else is on their fourth nylon one.
picks.flashyCheck: Tasteful. Heritage materials and no loud branding — it reads as 'knows quality,' not 'spent money.'
picks.productSearch: Filson small rugged twill duffel bag

picks.name: Dopp Kit
picks.bio: The detail that makes a hotel bathroom look like an adult uses it. A proper dopp kit keeps your travel grooming organized and off the counter, and a clean leather-trimmed one quietly upgrades every trip. Cheap insurance against being the guy with toiletries rattling around a plastic bag.
picks.flashyCheck: None. It's the unseen-but-noticed kind of put-together.

essays.title: The Only Three Pairs of Sunglasses You Need
essays.blurb: The default, the upgrade, and the value pick — and why the logo shades are a tell, not a flex.

essays.title: The Small Things People Actually Notice
essays.blurb: Wallet, belt, bag, frames. Why the details finish the look — and the rules nobody taught you (match the belt to the shoes).
